pmos

How LLMs Work — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Everyone on your team now ships features built on a large language model, and most product managers can use one fluently while having no picture of what happens between the prompt and the answer. This primer is that picture. It opens the box one layer at a time — how a model chops your text into tokens, turns those into embeddings (lists of numbers that carry meaning), and runs them through the transformer, the engine whose attention mechanism lets each word read the words around it. It then separates the two halves of a model's life that get confused constantly: training (the slow, expensive part where it learns) and inference (the fast part where it answers you, one token at a time). With the mechanics in hand, the behaviour stops being mysterious — why models hallucinate, why they forget, why prompting and retrieval change the output so much. It closes on the part that is actually your job: the cost, latency, and evaluation decisions a PM owns when building on top of one. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out — no machine-learning background assumed. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the field's teaching canon — Hugging Face, Google for Developers, and Microsoft Learn primary docs; Wikipedia for settled definitions; Jay Alammar's Illustrated Transformer and Sebastian Raschka for the mechanics; Databricks and the Prompt Engineering Guide for grounding and prompting; AssemblyAI and Product School for the PM lens. 15 verified sources.

What a large language model actually is

Strip away the chat interface and a large language model (an LLM — a program trained on enormous amounts of text to produce language) is doing one narrow thing: predicting the next chunk of text, given everything before it. Wikipedia defines it as "a neural network trained on a vast amount of text for natural language processing tasks, especially language generation," first trained "to predict the next word." A neural network is just a very large mathematical function with adjustable internal numbers; natural language processing (NLP) is the broader field of getting computers to work with human language.

Microsoft Learn offers the most useful framing for a PM: an LLM is "just two things" — "model weights — billions of numerical parameters learned during training that encode the model's knowledge," and the "architecture code" that runs them. The weights are the learned part; the architecture is the fixed machinery. Everything the model "knows" lives in those numbers, frozen at the moment training stopped. That single fact explains a surprising amount of its behaviour later in this primer — why it has a knowledge cutoff, why it cannot look anything up on its own, and why it sometimes states yesterday's news as though it never happened.

It is worth saying plainly what an LLM is not. It is not a database that stores and retrieves facts, and it is not a search engine. It is a pattern-completion engine that has read so much text that its best guess at "what comes next" is usually right. The rest of this primer is the machinery behind that guess.

Tokens — how models read text

A model never sees your words as words. The first step, run by a component called the tokenizer, is to break text into tokens — small pieces that may be a whole word, part of a word, or a single character. Hugging Face's teaching materials put the reason simply: tokenizers exist to "translate text into data that can be processed by the model," because "models can only process numbers." Each token maps to a number, an identifier in the model's fixed vocabulary, and the model works entirely on those numbers.

Why not just split on words? Hugging Face frames the camps. Word-based tokenization creates enormous vocabularies and chokes on any word it has never seen. Character-based tokenization keeps the vocabulary tiny but produces very long sequences that carry little meaning per piece. The winning compromise, used by nearly every modern model, is subword tokenization, built on "the principle that frequently used words should not be split into smaller subwords, but rare words should be decomposed into meaningful subwords." Common words stay whole; a rare word like "tokenization" might split into "token" and "ization." The payoff is "good coverage with small vocabularies, and close to no unknown tokens."

Tokens are not a footnote — they are the unit of money and memory. As Microsoft Learn notes, "Pricing is typically per-token," "Context windows are measured in tokens," and a rough rule of thumb is that "1 token ≈ ¾ of a word in English." When your engineer says a feature is "too expensive" or "too slow," the conversation is almost always really about token counts.

Embeddings — turning words into numbers that carry meaning

A token identifier on its own is just an arbitrary number, like a seat number — it says nothing about meaning. The model's next move is to convert each token into an embedding: a long list of numbers (a vector) that positions the token in a kind of meaning-space. Wikipedia defines a word embedding as "a real-valued vector that encodes the meaning of the word," where "words or phrases from the vocabulary are mapped to vectors of real numbers."

The load-bearing idea is geometric. As the same source puts it, "words that are closer in the vector space are expected to be similar in meaning" — a modern restatement of the old linguistic observation that "a word is characterized by the company it keeps." So "king" and "queen" land near each other, and far from "bicycle." The model is not told these relationships; it discovers them from how words co-occur across billions of sentences. The transformer architecture does this conversion with a simple lookup: as Wikipedia's transformer entry describes, "each integer token identifier is converted into an embedding vector via a lookup table." This is the moment raw text becomes math the rest of the network can reason over.

The transformer — the engine inside

Almost every LLM you have used runs on one architecture: the transformer. Wikipedia describes it as "a family of artificial neural network architectures based on the multi-head attention mechanism, in which text is converted to numerical representations called tokens." It arrived in a single 2017 research paper memorably titled "Attention Is All You Need."

What made it win is worth understanding, because it explains why this whole wave happened when it did. Earlier language models used recurrent neural networks (RNNs), which read text strictly left to right, one word at a time, carrying a running memory. That is slow and forgetful over long passages. The transformer, Wikipedia notes, "has no recurrent units," so it needs "less training time than earlier recurrent neural architectures (RNNs)," and crucially its "parallelizability was an important factor to its widespread use." Because it can process all tokens at once rather than in sequence, it scales onto the massive banks of graphics processing units (GPUs) that training requires. Hugging Face adds that the models you know — generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) families and others — were all "trained as language models ... on large amounts of raw text in a self-supervised fashion."

The diagram above is the whole pipeline in one line: text becomes tokens, tokens become embeddings, embeddings flow through a stack of transformer layers, and the final layer produces a probability for every possible next token. The single most important thing it shows is the loop at the bottom — the model does not write a sentence in one shot. It picks one token, adds it to the input, and runs the entire machine again. The next two sections open up the layer in the middle.

Attention — how words find their context

Attention is the idea the 2017 paper named in its title, and it is the heart of why transformers understand language so well. The problem it solves: a word's meaning depends on the words around it. Hugging Face describes attention layers as letting the model "pay specific attention to certain words in the sentence ... (and more or less ignore the others) when dealing with the representation of each word."

Google for Developers gives the example that makes it click. In the sentence "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired," what does "it" refer to? Self-attention answers "How much does each other token of input affect the interpretation of this token?" — and rates "animal" as most relevant to "it." Change one word, "tired" to "wide," and now "street" becomes the most relevant. The model is not following a grammar rule; it is weighing every word against every other.

Mechanically, Jay Alammar's widely cited Illustrated Transformer explains how the weighing is computed. For each token the model builds three learned vectors — "a Query vector, a Key vector, and a Value vector ... created by multiplying the embedding by three matrices." A useful mental model: the Query is what a word is looking for, each Key is what another word offers, and the Value is the information it passes along. A relevance score is "the dot product of the query vector with the key vector"; those scores are normalized so they sum to one, and each word's Value is blended in proportion to its score. Models run several of these in parallel — "multi-head" attention — which Alammar says "expands the model's ability to focus on different positions" and gives "multiple representation subspaces," so one head can track grammar while another tracks subject matter.

Layers and parameters — where the knowledge lives

One attention step is not enough; transformers stack many layers, and after each attention step comes a feed-forward step. Machine Learning Mastery traces a single token's journey through this stack: after attention produces "a context-enriched or context-aware token representation," feed-forward layers "further transform and refine the token features." Layer by layer, a token's representation gets richer — early layers tend to capture surface patterns, later layers more abstract meaning.

All of that machinery is governed by parameters — the adjustable numbers, also called weights, that were set during training. This is what "large" in "large language model" refers to. Google for Developers notes modern transformers contain "hundreds of billion or even trillions of parameters," and reports that, in practice, "Transformers with more parameters consistently outperform Transformers with fewer parameters." That observed relationship between size and capability — broadly, bigger has kept getting better — is much of why the industry has poured money into ever-larger models. The exact returns to scale are still being actively studied and debated, so treat "bigger is better" as a strong trend, not an iron law.

Pretraining — learning from the internet

Now we cross from how a finished model runs to how it is made. The first and most expensive stage is pretraining. Microsoft Learn describes it as where "the model learns the bulk of its knowledge": fed "massive amounts of text from the internet — books, articles, code, websites," it "learns to predict the next token given all previous tokens," a process that takes "thousands of GPUs for weeks or months" and produces a "base model."

The clever trick is that no humans label this data. Hugging Face calls it self-supervised learning, "in which the objective is automatically computed from the inputs of the model" — hide the next word, ask the model to guess it, correct it, repeat a few trillion times. Because the answer is just the next word already sitting in the text, the internet itself becomes the answer key. That is what let these models scale: the bottleneck became compute and data, not human labellers.

A base model, though, is not the helpful assistant you know. Microsoft Learn is blunt: it "is essentially a text-completion engine" that "isn't particularly useful as an assistant — it may continue your text in unexpected ways, generate harmful content, or simply ramble. It doesn't follow instructions reliably." Turning that raw engine into something that answers questions is the next stage.

Fine-tuning and RLHF — teaching the model manners

Fine-tuning is, in Hugging Face's words, "the training done after a model has been pretrained." It reuses everything the base model learned and nudges it toward a goal, which is why it needs far less data and compute than pretraining. For chat assistants this happens in two moves.

First, supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Microsoft Learn describes training the model "on curated datasets of high-quality conversations: human-written examples of ideal assistant behavior." These examples teach it the role of a helpful assistant — how to follow instructions, answer questions, and decline harmful requests.

Second, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), the step most associated with the jump in quality that made ChatGPT feel different. Sebastian Raschka breaks it into three steps: supervised fine-tuning on human-written responses; building a reward model by having humans rank competing outputs (the reward model's "next-token classification layer is substituted with a regression layer" so it outputs a single quality score); then adjusting the main model to earn higher scores, using a method called proximal policy optimization (PPO). Microsoft Learn notes RLHF "helps the model learn subtle quality distinctions that are hard to capture in static examples — like being concise vs. thorough." This is the difference between a model that can produce good text and one reliably tuned to prefer it.

Inference — how it writes an answer, one token at a time

Inference is the part you experience: the model generating a response. It is worth separating sharply from training, because they have completely different costs and constraints — training happens once and costs millions; inference happens on every request and is what your product bill is made of.

Microsoft Learn calls the mechanism "autoregressive generation." Your full prompt is tokenized and fed in; the model "produces a probability distribution over its vocabulary — predicting which token is most likely to come next"; one token is selected; that token "is appended to the full sequence, and the entire updated sequence is fed back into the model to generate the next token"; and this repeats until a stop signal. Machine Learning Mastery describes the final step inside each pass: a "linear output layer followed by a softmax" turns the model's internal state into probabilities, and the next token "is then selected — often the one with the highest probability, though sampling-based decoding strategies are also common."

The main dial product teams touch here is temperature. Microsoft Learn explains that at "Temperature = 0" the model "picks the most likely token each time" (more deterministic and repeatable), while higher temperatures sample "from a broader distribution" (more varied and creative). For reliable, structured tasks, lower is usually safer; for brainstorming, higher. One caveat the same source flags and many teams learn the hard way: "Even at temperature 0, LLMs are not fully deterministic" — so "don't design systems that depend on identical output for identical input." Because generation is one token at a time, longer answers take proportionally longer, which is why streaming the response as it is written is the norm.

The context window — the model's short-term memory

Every model has a hard limit on how much text it can consider at once, called the context window, and almost every quirk of "memory" traces back to it. Microsoft Learn describes it as the "fixed" maximum "number of tokens the model can handle," into which "everything must fit: your prompt, the conversation history, any injected context, and the tokens the model is generating as its response."

Two consequences matter for anyone building with LLMs. First, the model has no memory between calls on its own. As Microsoft Learn puts it, "Each API call is stateless. The model doesn't remember previous conversations unless you include them in the context window" — application programming interface (API) being the connection your software uses to call the model. The illusion of a model "remembering" your chat is your application quietly resending the history every turn. Second, the window is a budget, "your working memory budget — everything the model needs to know must fit within it." Modern models offer windows "from 4K to over 1M tokens," but it is always finite, and packing it fuller costs more and can crowd out the response. Managing what goes into that window — how much history, how much retrieved data — is one of the central design jobs of an LLM feature.

Why they hallucinate, and other quirks

The most consequential LLM behaviour for a product is hallucination. Wikipedia defines it as "a response generated by AI that contains false or misleading information presented as fact." The crucial point for a PM is that this is not a bug to be patched away — it falls directly out of the mechanism described above.

Here is why. The model was trained to always predict a plausible next token. As Wikipedia explains, that objective "incentivizes GPT models to 'give a guess' about what the next word is, even when they lack information," because training "rewards guessing over acknowledging uncertainty." The model has no built-in sense of whether its fluent output is true — it is, in the words of one researcher quoted there, "indifferent to the truth of their outputs." Worse, hallucinations sound authoritative; the same source notes models have fabricated complete citations and produced "a very impressive-sounding answer that's just dead wrong." A PM building on LLMs should treat confident fluency and factual accuracy as two separate things.

The other limitations cluster naturally around the mechanics. Because the weights are frozen at training time, the model has "no real-time knowledge" past its cutoff. Because each call is stateless, it has "no persistent memory." Because it generates tokens rather than computing, it has "limited math and logic." And because it only emits text, it has "no ability to act" — it cannot send an email or query a database by itself. Microsoft Learn lists exactly these, and the framing matters: "Many of these limitations are exactly what agents are designed to address." The next section covers the most common fixes.

Prompting and RAG — getting better output

You change an LLM's output without retraining it in two main ways. The first is prompting. The Prompt Engineering Guide defines prompt engineering as "designing effective prompts to instruct the model to perform a desired task," noting that "the quality of results depends on how much information you provide it and how well-crafted the prompt is." A powerful variant is few-shot prompting — showing the model a handful of worked examples — which triggers "in-context learning, which is the ability of language models to learn tasks given a few demonstrations." This is genuinely surprising: the model picks up the pattern from examples in the prompt itself, with no weights changed.

The second is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), the standard way to make a general model knowledgeable about your specific data. Databricks defines it as "a hybrid AI framework that bolsters large language models (LLMs) by combining them with external, up-to-date data sources." When a question comes in, the system first retrieves "semantically similar documents" from your own knowledge base and pastes them into the prompt, so the model can "produce more accurate, context-aware answers grounded in enterprise-specific or up-to-date data." Because the facts now sit in the context window rather than having to be baked into the weights, RAG "attempts to mitigate the risk of responding with ... hallucinations" and works "without needing to customize the model." It is the most common answer to both the knowledge-cutoff and hallucination problems — though, as the broader literature is careful to note, it reduces hallucination rather than eliminating it.

What this means for PMs

The mechanics land on a handful of product decisions that are squarely a PM's job. The first is the adaptation choice. Product School lays out the three levers in increasing order of effort: prompting via "system prompts," retrieval ("combine retrieval and generative models" for "highly contextual information"), and fine-tuning on custom datasets. The practical rule that follows from this primer: reach for prompting first, RAG when the model needs facts it does not have, and fine-tuning only when you need to change how the model behaves, not just what it knows.

The second is economics, and here tokens come back. Product School notes "Larger, more powerful models may incur higher usage fees," typically charging "per token, request, or compute time." Every design choice — how much history you keep, how verbose the output, how big the retrieved context — is a line item. AssemblyAI underlines the scale of the underlying investment: pretraining a frontier model "like PaLM" has run "10 to 20 million US dollars," which is exactly why almost no product trains its own and most build on a provider's API instead.

The third is reliability and evaluation. Because hallucination is structural, AssemblyAI frames acceptable behaviour as a trio — helpfulness, truthfulness, and harmlessness — and a model meeting them is "aligned" with human values. For a PM that translates into concrete work: design the feature assuming the model will sometimes be confidently wrong, show sources or confidence where it matters, keep a human in the loop for high-stakes decisions, and build an evaluation set so "did our change help?" is a measured question rather than a vibe. Product School adds the governance reminder that operations must "comply with relevant data protection regulations such as GDPR" — the General Data Protection Regulation — which is sharply relevant once user data flows into prompts.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Data science models (the wider menu) — LLMs are one corner of machine learning; regression, classification, clustering, and recommenders are the everyday workhorses behind most predictive features.
  • AI agents — wrapping an LLM with tools, memory, and a loop so it can take actions, not just generate text; the natural next step from "what LLMs can't do."
  • Vector databases and embeddings search — the storage and similarity-search layer that makes retrieval-augmented generation work in production.
  • Prompt engineering and evaluation — the deeper craft of designing prompts and the test sets that tell you whether a change actually improved output.
  • Model fine-tuning and RLHF in practice — when and how teams actually customize a model's weights, and what it costs versus prompting or retrieval.
  • AI ethics, bias, and safety — how training data shapes behaviour, where bias enters, and what "alignment" and responsible deployment require.

References

  1. learn.microsoft.com ·cited· (T1)

    Microsoft Learn frames an LLM as "just two things": "Model weights — billions of numerical parameters learned during training that encode the model's knowledge" and the "architecture code" (typically a Transformer) that runs them. It stresses the PM-facing consequence of tokens: "Pricing is typically per-token," "Context windows are measured in tokens," and "Longer prompts use more tokens, cost more, and leave less room for the model's response."

  2. developers.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google's Machine Learning Crash Course explains self-attention as answering "How much does each other token of input affect the interpretation of this token?" — in "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired," attention rates "animal" as most relevant to "it," but swapping "tired" for "wide" makes "street" more relevant. It notes transformers contain "hundreds of billion or even trillions of parameters," and that more parameters "consistently outperform" fewer.

  3. huggingface.co ·cited· (T1)

    Hugging Face's tokenizers chapter explains that tokenizers "translate text into data that can be processed by the model" because "models can only process numbers." Word-based tokenization creates huge vocabularies and unknown tokens; character-based makes long sequences with little meaning; subword tokenization follows "the principle that frequently used words should not be split into smaller subwords, but rare words should be decomposed into meaningful subwords," giving "good coverage with small vocabularies, and close to no unknown tokens."

  4. huggingface.co ·cited· (T1)

    Hugging Face explains LLMs are trained with self-supervised learning — "the objective is automatically computed from the inputs of the model," so human labels are not needed. Attention layers let a model "pay specific attention to certain words in the sentence ... (and more or less ignore the others)." Pretraining trains "from scratch: the weights are randomly initialized"; fine-tuning is "the training done after a model has been pretrained," needing far less data.

  5. jalammar.github.io ·cited· (T1)

    Jay Alammar's Illustrated Transformer breaks self-attention into three learned vectors per token: "a Query vector, a Key vector, and a Value vector ... created by multiplying the embedding by three matrices." A score is "the dot product of the query vector with the key vector"; scores are softmax-normalized, then each value is weighted and summed. Multi-head attention "expands the model's ability to focus on different positions" and gives "multiple representation subspaces."

  6. magazine.sebastianraschka.com ·cited· (T1)

    Sebastian Raschka breaks RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) into three steps: supervised fine-tuning on human-written responses; a reward model trained on human rankings of model outputs (its "next-token classification layer is substituted with a regression layer" outputting one score); then policy optimization with "proximal policy optimization (PPO)." Pretraining uses raw "next-word prediction"; supervised fine-tuning uses "instruction-output pairs" on "much smaller datasets."

  7. databricks.com ·cited· (T1)

    Databricks defines retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as "a hybrid AI framework that bolsters large language models (LLMs) by combining them with external, up-to-date data sources," retrieving "semantically similar documents" from a vector database and adding them to the prompt to "produce more accurate, context-aware answers grounded in enterprise-specific or up-to-date data." It "attempts to mitigate the risk of responding with ... hallucinations" and works "without needing to customize the model."

  8. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines a large language model as "a neural network trained on a vast amount of text for natural language processing tasks, especially language generation," first pretrained "to predict the next word" then fine-tuned via techniques like RLHF. It names hallucination directly: models "generate syntactically fluent text that appears factually sound, but is internally inconsistent with training data or factually incorrect."

  9. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines a word embedding as "a real-valued vector that encodes the meaning of the word," where "words or phrases from the vocabulary are mapped to vectors of real numbers." The load-bearing principle: "words that are closer in the vector space are expected to be similar in meaning," reflecting that "a word is characterized by the company it keeps."

  10. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia describes the transformer as "a family of artificial neural network architectures based on the multi-head attention mechanism, in which text is converted to numerical representations called tokens," each "contextualized within the scope of the context window with other (unmasked) tokens." Born in the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need," it "has no recurrent units," needing "less training time than earlier recurrent neural architectures (RNNs)"; its "parallelizability" drove adoption at scale.

  11. machinelearningmastery.com ·cited· (T2)

    Machine Learning Mastery traces a token's path: tokenize to an identifier, add "information about the position of that token in the sequence," pass through multi-headed attention to get "a context-enriched or context-aware token representation," then feed-forward layers that "further transform and refine the token features." A final "linear output layer followed by a softmax" produces logits then probabilities; the next token "is then selected — often the one with the highest probability, though sampling-based decoding strategies are also common."

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines an AI hallucination as "a response generated by AI that contains false or misleading information presented as fact." The mechanism is structural: predicting the next word "incentivizes GPT models to 'give a guess' about what the next word is, even when they lack information," because training "rewards guessing over acknowledging uncertainty." Hallucinations sound authoritative — models have fabricated citations and "a very impressive-sounding answer that's just dead wrong."

  13. promptingguide.ai ·cited· (T2)

    The Prompt Engineering Guide defines prompt engineering as "designing effective prompts to instruct the model to perform a desired task," where "the quality of results depends on how much information you provide it and how well-crafted the prompt is." Few-shot prompting (showing a few labeled examples) enables "in-context learning, which is the ability of language models to learn tasks given a few demonstrations."

  14. assemblyai.com ·cited· (T2)

    AssemblyAI's PM guide flags the economics: pretraining a model "like PaLM" runs "10 to 20 million US dollars," and "the total amount of available training data might be the true bottleneck." It warns LLMs "may exhibit undesirable behavior, such as reproducing harmful or biased content, or generating hallucinations by fabricating false facts," and frames acceptable behavior as helpfulness, truthfulness, and harmlessness — a model meeting these is "aligned" with human values.

  15. productschool.com ·cited· (T2)

    Product School maps the three ways PMs adapt an LLM to a domain: prompting via "system prompts," fine-tuning on custom datasets, and RAG to "combine retrieval and generative models" for "highly contextual information" from organization-specific data. On tradeoffs: "Larger, more powerful models may incur higher usage fees," often charging "per token, request, or compute time," while smaller models can run faster, and operations must "comply with relevant data protection regulations such as GDPR."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_how-llms-work.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
